mappings.rationaleAI: A 700-verse dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna synthesizing dharma, karma, moksha, Vedanta, Samkhya, and Yoga traditions addresses post-Vedic Sanskrit literature (B.1.b), Hindu philosophy (B.3), views about God through Krishna's divine revelation (C.1.a), karman and samsara (C.1.c.ii), and the three margas of duties, knowledge, and devotion (C.1.c.iii).

One of the great Sanskrit epics narrating Prince Rama's adventures through themes of duty, devotion, and cosmic struggle between good and evil, remaining a living sacred scripture for hundreds of millions of Hindus — directly relevant to post-Vedic epics (B.1.b), Hindu mythology (B.2), and religious expression in the arts (C.4.b).

Examines the doctrine of karma, samsara, and paths to moksha across Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist traditions, covering diverse interpretations of free will, intention, renunciation, and liberation — directly addressing karman, samsara, and transmigration (C.1.c.ii) and Jain beliefs and practices (D.1.c).

Vatsyayana's Gupta-era treatise treats kama (pleasure) alongside dharma and artha as the three legitimate aims of Hindu life, reflecting sophisticated social conduct and domestic practice — relevant to post-Vedic Sanskrit literature (B.1.b), the three margas and paths of Hindu life (C.1.c.iii), and Hindu social practices and institutions (C.3).

Al-Biruni's Kitab al-Hind (c. 1017-1030) provides the first systematic Muslim scholarly account of Hindu religion, science, and everyday life in comprehensive detail — relevant to the age of bhakti and Hindu-Islamic interaction (A.8) and as a primary external source documenting Hindu beliefs and practices (C.1).

Rabindranath Tagore's Gitanjali devotional poems, his educational reform at Visva-Bharati, renunciation of his British knighthood after Amritsar, and composition of India's national anthem connect to the modern period of Hinduism (A.9), sacred literature in Indian regional languages through Bengali (B.1.c), and ethical, social, and nationalist movements in modern Hinduism (C.2.f).

Indian mathematical development originated in Vedic religious texts requiring geometric precision for altar construction (Sulbasutras) and astronomical calculations for determining Hindu festival dates — directly relevant to sacrifice and worship (C.3.a), sacred times (C.3.b), and cultural expressions of Hindu values (C.4).

Maharaja Jai Singh II's Jantar Mantar observatory produced astrological tables significant to Hindu religious and social life, merging Indian and Persian astronomical traditions for measuring celestial events — relevant to sacred times and places (C.3.b) and cultural expressions of Hindu values and ideas (C.4).

Ashoka's conversion from the Brahmanical tradition to Buddhism and his subsequent promotion of Buddhist principles across the largest South Asian empire exemplifies the heterodox period's challenges to Brahmanism by reformers and ascetic groups (A.4).

Akbar's religious tolerance policies and syncretic Indo-Islamic cultural production shaped Hindu-Muslim interaction during the age of bhakti (A.8), while the empire's legacy in modern South Asian identity connects to nationalist movements in modern Hinduism (C.2.f).
Scholarly debate over the Roman cult's connections to the Vedic god Mitra and Zoroastrian Mithra illuminates the Indo-European religious roots shared with early Hinduism (A.1).
